paragraphs.text: A near-gale screams the loneliness of
an overgrown boulder that's a whipping post for weather
. It's spring, sunny — and about 45 degrees with wind chill. Winter jackets are zipped to the neck. Gloves would be nice. And you start to wonder how
dressed in only cormorant feathers could have survived alone for 18 years in
where even the ravaged rocks tell you they got a raw deal. "They found her right around here," shouts
, an archeologist and environmental planner for the
. Tall, genial and sporting a gray beard circa
is straining to be heard over a thundering wind that delivers the sharp tang of seal droppings from the throng of flippers on the rocks below.
is home to a radar and missile tracking installation, but that's not why
has marooned himself here every week for the last 15 years.
He's been trying to solve a mystery, to rescue from a century and a half of fable,
who came to be known as
the Lone Woman of San Nicolas
waves his arm to indicate the area where she was discovered in 1853: a scene of rolling dunes, blown down to the bedrock in places and upholstered in 2,000-year-old abalone shells.
left the shells here at what is now one of the richest archeologist sites in
They are reminders of 8,000 years of indigenous settlement on
. An arid plateau ringed by cliffs and thick tangles of kelp,
was protected from invaders through the years by its lack of safe harbors.
Then, in the early 19th century, the world's lust for otter coats led
. They were supposed to say for a few weeks, but the outing turned into a year.
are said to have gone on a rampage, wiping out most of
By 1835 a population that once numbered 300 had shrunk to just seven.
had been self-sufficient since Europeans were scratching cave walls, the
decided to sponsor a rescue operation — or at least that's the conventional wisdom.
had a high fatality rate among
, who had no immunity to European diseases.
, arriving off its rugged northern tip.
set out across the dunes, rounded up six of
bucked offshore in a swelling storm,
was "away in the mountains," while the most popular explanation would become the defining, mythic tale. In this version,
but then, as it heads for open waters, realizes that
has been left behind. Desperate, she plunges over the rail into rolling waves to save

has scoured the literature, artifacts and museums to separate facts from embroidery. "The story of her jumping overboard does not show up until the 1880s," he says. "By then the Victorian era is well underway, and literature takes on a flowery, even romantic flavor
The more likely scenario: With the storm threatening to slam
panicked and turned the rudder back toward
without doing much of a head count. "The earliest firsthand accounts simply state that she was mistakenly left behind," says
EITHER way, people in the coastal settlements knew that
had been abandoned. Why were there no rescue attempts?
had intended to go back but had orders to take a shipment of lumber to
had foundered at the entrance to
Most accounts blame a lack of available ships in the mid-1830s for the failure of anyone to step in and make the crossing.
, who had assembled a stash of shipping data from a former job with the
, didn't buy it. He investigated further with local marine historians.
There were plenty of boats big enough to get out here," he says. "It didn't take a huge boat. It's just: Was there really reason enough to go back for
to scour the horizon for a human trace and began a long confrontation with a social animal's deepest fears.
says that sometimes when he's out there poking around amid the artifacts he can feel some of the isolation that must have haunted her as she wandered the paths
would never amble again. "On the west end of
, where you can't see any buildings or people, all of a sudden you get this sense of what it's like to be alone . . . the overwhelming sounds of the surf, the gulls crying."

is sure that "the hardest part would have been the loneliness, the wondering if someone were coming back for me, or if I was left for good . . . after some time you probably have to give up hope. That day [was] probably the worst of all."

EVEN the gods tremble in the presence of absence. Creation stories abound with deities who feel the urge to liven the cosmic black hole with company. The desolation of separation saves its best shots, though, for the social animal.
"It's loneliness that makes the loudest noise," wrote philosopher

, it no doubt thundered up the arroyos and over the thrashing surf, drowning out all with the echo of what was no longer there. Humans are adaptable creatures, but how do you begin to adjust to a life without a single other human in it?
"It wouldn't surprise me if she went through the stage of grief that people go through when they lose their loved ones, coming finally to a stage of acceptance," says
who has studied the effects of isolation on researchers in
in the 19th century may as well have been
. Exiled well east from the northern and southern
is ideal for solitary confinement. On a rare clear day,
. But most of the year the relentless fog and clouds that cloaks
would have shut her off from even a glimpse of the rest of the world.

Prolonged isolation can cause serious psychological and even physical havoc for a species designated for contact. "When you get cut off from the group, you're much more likely to feel sad, blue, depressed, hopeless," says
, a brain imagery specialist who teaches at
Loneliness isn't an affliction of the meek. It's a basic human state, the biochemical prod for us to follow our social mandate, which is good for species propagation and vital gray matter.
"We're programmed to be connected with other people,"

says. Talking and smiling and simply being with other people "regulates certain systems in our brains and makes them work better." A lack of social stimulation can cause dendrites, the conduits through which information flows to brain cells, to shrink and disappear.
— huts, tools, bowls, necklaces — but no hands, eyes or voices,

had to find a way through a life unwitnessed and unshared. What thoughts could have overridden the despair to keep her brain healthy and prevent a descent into madness or a leap off one of numerous nearby cliffs?
only has to hear of the woman's fate to be drawn in. "She needed to keep hope about connection again," he says. "She probably was able to develop her own rich internal life."
At first, one of her biggest challenges would have been overcoming certain Nicoleño taboos that would have made essential survival tasks off-limits, says

. "In her culture, activities were ascribed to male or female. It was probably taboo for her to touch a fish hook, [or] a spear for catching seals. She would really have had to grapple with that and overcome it."
One thing that might have helped her cope with the social void was that
was by no means absent of life. Close your eyes on her part of

, and you are far from alone. Cormorants squawk. Hordes of sea elephants and sea lions debate the fine points of beachfront property with the restraint of a soccer mob in full riot. There were also dogs on

. "If you're connected to your animals, you're connected. You can talk to them, interact with them, feed them, have a relationship with them. The other thing that does is it gives her purpose. She could say, 'Hey, we gotta eat today, we have to explore today.'"
The pickings on land were sparse — only a few roots and tubers. But, as
discovered from digging through archeological sites, nature had stocked the local waters with a supply of more than 100 species of fish. Unlike the marooned fishermen,
had the skills from her culture to make use of the bounty.
She probably continued to eat as she had with

, beside a fire in a protected part of a hut framed by whale bones and covered with seal skins. She could have used marcasite stones to spark a flame, but "it would have been easier to keep one going all the time," says
. "A typical meal would most likely be fish or shellfish, probably prepared every way known to man: raw, boiled, grilled, smoked. Maybe some bulbs too."
On the island's northern edge, near one of the springs where
got her fresh water, there's a mountain of evidence for her menu, and how she fashioned it.

pulls off a dirt road and parks his military-issue, cream-colored van alongside the mother lode of middens. Twenty acres of sand dunes loom 120 feet above the beach, blanketed as far as the eye can see with tools, utensils and seafood leftovers — as if they were plopped down an hour ago.
"The whole culture is just kind of laying here, if you know how to read it," says
Tiptoeing uphill between shells and artifacts to avoid crunching history,
leans over and picks up part of an abalone shell with a doughnut hole-sized gap in the middle. "It's a fish hook blank," he says.
would have worked it down from the hole into a j-shaped hook.
A prize 10-inch stone pestle more than 1,000 years old sits undisturbed on the sand.
picks up a bone tool of a type

would have used for prying abalone meat from its shell. Most of the materials date from AD 700 to 800. The dinnerware is here too: mortars that served the fare. "Seasoned archeologists say they see more in one day here than they have in a 20-year career,"
says, grinning atop one of 538 archeological sites on

fished and foraged the tidal pools and grasses. She may have hunted sea mammals or found them already dead on the beach. A 2-foot-long section of vertebrae hints at part of the Lone Woman's diet. The bones are from a dolphin spine.
speculates that the hill was used for spotting dolphin schools offshore.
forged on — five, 10, 17 years — alone but not entirely forgotten. The journal of
during the Gold Rush fever of 1849 [1890], for instance, described
in search of gull eggs and sea otters. He didn't find any eggs, but he did stumble onto footprints on the beach and pieces of dried seal blubber hanging from poles. Tantalized,
later in the year and discovered more clues: fresh footprints and a basket someone had placed in a bush.
Rough seas drove him back to
. He was now convinced, though, that
Fired up by the new evidence,
to go back and find her. His team arrived in 1853. One of his men,
, was combing the northwest dunes when he saw what he said looked like "a small black object about the size of a crow, which seemed to be in motion."
As he approached, the crow grew and changed colors. In fact, the greenish feathers came from a much larger bird, a cormorant, and this one seemed to have arms, not wings.
I didn't know if she would bite or scratch
She had a brush fence about 2 feet high to break the wind, and right in front she sat facing me. She was skinning a seal before I came up to her.
reported passing two growling dogs on his way up to the dune. silenced them with a command. She put the seal aside and gestured for
to share roots around the fire in her hut.
, who couldn't understand the woman's language. Still, she seemed ecstatic to have company. Having no doubt stored plenty of topics for conversation, she "jabbered to herself,"

was of medium height, but rather thick," he reported. "She must have been about 50 years old, but she was still strong and active. Her face was pleasing as she was continually smiling. Her teeth were entire but worn to the gums."
Making friends easily, she joined in with chores around the camp, as

spent three weeks hunting otter. When it came time to leave, she gathered every scrap of her possessions — a necklace, fish hooks, a bone needle, awl, stone mortar rope, and all available food, including a rotting seal's head with brains spilling out. And then the winds that had swirled through millennia of hut-building and fishing parties snapped the sails of
the last native of the Channel Islands
to the world beyond the horizon. She clapped and danced without a trace of self-consciousness — one benefit of isolation — as
News of the rescue raced through the small town of
. Residents flocked to see her at
. But no local Indian could decipher her dialect. Some scholars would later contend that this proved she was a descendant of
recently investigated, focusing on four words
spoke to one of her rescuers.
The linguist's study suggests that the woman's language was similar to those of natives closer to
The last of a once-thriving culture
was gregarious and clearly enjoyed the attention, signing and dancing for her audiences. She marveled at horses, ox carts, clothes and mostly the food, gorging on fresh fruit.
But in a twist out of Greek tragedy,
named Juana Maria came down with dysentery and within two weeks of leaving
by herself but only two weeks when she got back to 'civilization,'" says
Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History
and an expert on local Native American cultures. "There's a lesson there."
A legacy too. Surrounded by the tools, pendants and household appliances of the society that helped one rugged individual to survive,
knows there's something even worse than the loneliness of a single soul. It's a culture that is missing a part of itself. As he stands jacket flapping on the dunes, he knows that
includes a photo of an Indian woman whose identity remains a mystery. Some say she may be
the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island
, but experts say this isn't true.
